quote: pekor follows the finite clause nep ka e=nukar 'you see something' and connects to the main clause e=yki 'you behave/act'. The presupposition is that nothing is actually visible — the comparison is counterfactual. Satō (2008: 56) uses this example to contrast pekor with noyne: noyne would instead report a genuine inference from evidence.

quote: The subject (a hen) is implicit. kor is the transitive verb 'have'; noyne is the inferential clause-linker that marks the entire first clause [tane tamanko kor] 'now having an egg' as inferred rather than directly witnessed. okokokse is onomatopoeic 'kok-kok' used as a sound verb; hawe as 'there is the voice/sound of' closes the main clause.

quote: pakno marks the degree of the father's wealth as the comparison standard. The prenominal clause [a=ona pakno nispa isam] 'to whose extent/degree no rich man exists' modifies the head nispa 'rich man'. The strategy amounts to a comparative without a dedicated comparative particle: no other rich man exists to the extent/degree of my father. See the full relativization analysis in the prenominal chapter.
